title: Definitions
content: Key definitions: (1)(a) 'access' means gaining entry into, instructing or communicating with logical, arithmetical, or memory function resources of computer; (d) 'affixing electronic signature' means adoption of any methodology by person for purpose of authenticating an electronic record; (f) 'asymmetric crypto system' means system of secure key pair consisting of private key for creating digital signature and public key to verify digital signature; (i) 'communication device' includes cell phones, personal digital assistants, or combination of both; (j) 'computer' means electronic, magnetic, optical or other high-speed data processing device which performs logical, arithmetic and memory functions; (k) 'computer network' means interconnection of one or more computers; (l) 'computer resource' means computer, communication device, network, data, database or software; (nb) 'cyber café' means facility from where access to internet is offered in ordinary course of business to public; (r) 'electronic record' means data, record, image generated, received, stored, transmitted in electronic form; (ta) 'electronic signature' means authentication of electronic record by subscriber by means of electronic technique; (w) 'intermediary' means person who on behalf of another person receives, stores, transmits any electronic record including telecom, network, internet service providers, web-hosting providers, search engines, online payment sites, online-auction sites, online market places, and cyber cafes.

title: Authentication of Electronic Records
content: Subject to provisions of Section 3A, any subscriber may authenticate an electronic record by affixing his electronic signature. Authentication of electronic record shall be effected by use of asymmetric crypto system and hash function which envelop and transform the initial electronic record into another electronic record. Any person can verify the electronic record by receiving such electronic signature and public key of subscriber. The hash function is algorithm mapping electronic record to another electronic record, and is considered unique if computationally infeasible to derive original record from hash, derive two records producing same hash, or modify record without changing hash.

title: Electronic Signature
content: A subscriber may authenticate any electronic record by such electronic signature or electronic authentication technique which: (a) is considered reliable; and (b) may be specified in the Second Schedule. Electronic signature/authentication shall be considered reliable if: (i) signature creation data is linked to signatory and no other person at time of signing; (ii) signature creation data was under control of signatory at time of signing; (iii) any alteration to electronic signature after affixing is detectable; (iv) any alteration to information made after its authentication is detectable. Central Government may prescribe procedure for authentication, including manner of identification and submission of information.

title: Legal Recognition of Electronic Records
content: Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be in writing or typewritten or printed form, then notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such information or matter is: (a) rendered or made available in electronic form; and (b) accessible so as to be usable for subsequent reference. This section provides legal equivalence between electronic records and paper-based records and is fundamental for digital transactions.

title: Legal Recognition of Electronic Signatures
content: Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be authenticated by affixing signature or any document shall be signed or bear signature of any person then, notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such information or matter is authenticated by means of electronic signature affixed in manner as may be prescribed by Central Government. This provides legal equivalence to electronic signatures with wet signatures.

title: Use of Electronic Records and Signatures in Government
content: Where any law provides for: (a) filing of any form, application or document with government in a manner/format; (b) issue or grant of any license, permit, sanction or approval by government in particular manner; (c) receipt or payment of money in particular manner - then notwithstanding anything in such law, such requirement shall be deemed satisfied if filing, issue, grant, receipt or payment is effected by means of electronic form/record. This enables e-governance and digital service delivery by government agencies.

title: Validity of Contracts Formed Through Electronic Means
content: Where in a contract formation, the communication of proposals, acceptance of proposals, revocation of proposals and acceptances are expressed in electronic form or by means of an electronic record, such contract shall not be deemed to be unenforceable solely on the ground that such electronic form or means was used for that purpose. This section provides legal validity to e-contracts and online transactions including click-wrap and browse-wrap agreements.

title: Penalty and Compensation for Damage to Computer, System, etc.
content: If any person without permission of owner/person in charge of computer: (a) accesses/secures access to computer/network/resource; (b) downloads, copies or extracts any data/database including information held in removable storage medium; (c) introduces or causes to be introduced any computer contaminant or virus; (d) damages or causes damage to any computer/system/network/data/database; (e) disrupts or causes disruption of any computer; (f) denies or causes denial of access to authorized persons; (g) provides assistance to any person to facilitate access in contravention of this Act; (h) charges services availed by one person to another's account by tampering/manipulating; (i) destroys, deletes or alters any information residing in computer - he shall be liable to pay damages by way of compensation to person so affected. Compensation may extend to Rs. 5 crore as adjudicating officer deems fit. Second proviso excludes liability for good faith verification of computer security under lawful contract.

title: Compensation for Failure to Protect Data
content: Where a body corporate, possessing, dealing or handling any sensitive personal data or information in a computer resource which it owns, controls or operates, is negligent in implementing and maintaining reasonable security practices and procedures and thereby causes wrongful loss or wrongful gain to any person, such body corporate shall be liable to pay damages by way of compensation to the person so affected. 'Body corporate' includes company, firm, sole proprietorship or association of individuals engaged in commercial/professional activities. 'Reasonable security practices' include ISO 27001 or as prescribed. 'Sensitive personal data' includes password, financial information, health records, sexual orientation, medical history, biometric data. IT (Reasonable Security Practices) Rules, 2011 prescribe the standards.

title: Tampering with Computer Source Documents
content: Whoever knowingly or intentionally conceals, destroys or alters or intentionally or knowingly causes another to conceal, destroy or alter any computer source code used for a computer, computer programme, computer system or computer network, when the computer source code is required to be kept or maintained by law for the time being in force, shall be punishable with imprisonment up to three years, or with fine which may extend to two lakh rupees, or with both. 'Computer source code' means the listing of programmes, computer commands, design and layout and programme analysis of computer resource in any form.

title: Computer Related Offences
content: If any person, dishonestly or fraudulently, does any act referred to in Section 43, he shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years or with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees or with both. This elevates Section 43 civil wrongs to criminal offences when committed dishonestly or fraudulently. The distinction between Section 43 (civil) and Section 66 (criminal) lies in the element of dishonesty or fraud.

title: STRUCK DOWN - Punishment for Offensive Messages
content: This section was STRUCK DOWN as UNCONSTITUTIONAL by the Supreme Court in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015). The section had provided for punishment for sending offensive messages through communication service with imprisonment up to three years and fine. The Supreme Court held Section 66A violated Article 19(1)(a) (freedom of speech) as it was vague and overbroad, creating chilling effect on free speech. Terms like 'grossly offensive', 'menacing', and 'annoyance' were undefined and allowed arbitrary application. This remains the landmark case for online free speech in India.

title: Punishment for Dishonestly Receiving Stolen Computer Resource
content: Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen computer resource or communication device knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen computer resource or communication device, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years or with fine which may extend to rupees one lakh or with both.

title: Punishment for Identity Theft
content: Whoever, fraudulently or dishonestly make use of the electronic signature, password or any other unique identification feature of any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to rupees one lakh. This covers identity theft in digital environment including use of another's credentials for unauthorized access.

title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation by Using Computer Resource
content: Whoever, by means of any communication device or computer resource cheats by personating, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to one lakh rupees. This addresses phishing, spoofing, and online impersonation for cheating.

title: Punishment for Violation of Privacy
content: Whoever, intentionally or knowingly captures, publishes or transmits the image of a private area of any person without his or her consent, under circumstances violating the privacy of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to three years or with fine not exceeding two lakh rupees, or with both. 'Private area' means naked or undergarment clad genitals, pubic area, buttocks or female breast. 'Transmit' includes download, upload or create text/image. 'Circumstances violating privacy' means where person has reasonable expectation of privacy.

title: Punishment for Cyber Terrorism
content: (1) Whoever with intent to threaten unity, integrity, security or sovereignty of India or to strike terror in people or any section of people by: (i) denying or causing denial of access to person authorized to access computer resource; or (ii) attempting to penetrate or access computer resource without authorization, or exceeding authorized access; and by means of such conduct causes or is likely to cause death or injuries to persons or damage to or destruction of property or disrupts or knowing that it is likely to cause damage or disruption of supplies or services essential to life of community or adversely affect critical information infrastructure - commits cyber terrorism. (2) Whoever commits or conspires to commit cyber terrorism shall be punishable with imprisonment which may extend to imprisonment for life.

title: Punishment for Publishing or Transmitting Obscene Material
content: Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in the electronic form, any material which is lascivious or appeals to the prurient interest or if its effect is such as to tend to deprave and corrupt persons who are likely, having regard to all relevant circumstances, to read, see or hear the matter contained or embodied in it, shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees and in the event of second or subsequent conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years and also with fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees.

title: Punishment for Publishing or Transmitting Sexually Explicit Act
content: Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in the electronic form any material which contains sexually explicit act or conduct shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years and with fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees and in the event of second or subsequent conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years and also with fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees. Exception for legitimate scientific/educational purposes.

title: Punishment for Publishing or Transmitting Child Pornography
content: Whoever: (a) publishes/transmits in electronic form material depicting children engaged in sexually explicit act/conduct; (b) creates, seeks, collects, stores, downloads, advertises, promotes, exchanges or distributes such material; (c) cultivates, entices or induces children to online relationship with one or more children for sexually explicit act; (d) facilitates abusing children online; (e) records in any electronic form own abuse or that of others pertaining to sexually explicit act with children - shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years and fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees, and second conviction with imprisonment up to seven years and fine up to ten lakh rupees.

title: Power to Issue Directions for Interception or Monitoring
content: Where the Central Government or a State Government or any of its officers specially authorized by the Central Government or the State Government, as the case may be, is satisfied that it is necessary or expedient so to do, in the interest of sovereignty or integrity of India, defense of India, security of the State, friendly relations with foreign States or public order or for preventing incitement to the commission of any cognizable offence relating to above or for investigation of any offence, it may by order, direct any agency of the appropriate Government to intercept, monitor or decrypt or cause to be intercepted or monitored or decrypted any information generated, transmitted, received or stored in any computer resource. Subscriber or intermediary or any person in charge shall extend all facilities and technical assistance. Procedure prescribed under IT (Procedure and Safeguards for Interception, Monitoring and Decryption of Information) Rules, 2009. Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy read in requirement for judicial oversight.

title: Power to Issue Directions for Blocking for Public Access
content: Where the Central Government or any of its officers specially authorized by it is satisfied that it is necessary or expedient so to do, in the interest of sovereignty and integrity of India, defense of India, security of the State, friendly relations with foreign States or public order or for preventing incitement to the commission of any cognizable offence relating to above, it may by order direct any agency of the Government or intermediary to block for access by the public or cause to be blocked for access by the public any information generated, transmitted, received, stored or hosted in any computer resource. Intermediary failing to comply punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine. IT (Procedure and Safeguards for Blocking for Access of Information by Public) Rules, 2009 govern the procedure. Shreya Singhal mandated that intermediary must receive written reasoned order before blocking.

title: Power to Authorize to Monitor Traffic Data
content: The Central Government may, to enhance cyber security and for identification, analysis and prevention of intrusion or spread of computer contaminant in the country, by notification in the Official Gazette, authorize any agency of the Government to monitor and collect traffic data or information generated, transmitted, received or stored in any computer resource. Intermediary or person in charge of computer resource shall extend technical assistance and provide access. Traffic data includes data identifying communication's origin, destination, route, time, date, size, duration, or type of underlying service.

title: Protected System
content: The appropriate Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, declare any computer resource which directly or indirectly affects the facility of Critical Information Infrastructure, to be a protected system. Central Government shall prescribe the information security practices and procedures for such protected system. Any person who secures or attempts to secure access to a protected system in contravention of provisions shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years and shall also be liable to fine.

title: Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In)
content: The Central Government shall appoint an agency of the Government to be called the Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In). CERT-In shall serve as national agency for performing following functions: (a) collection, analysis and dissemination of information on cyber incidents; (b) forecast and alerts of cyber security incidents; (c) emergency measures for handling cyber security incidents; (d) coordination of cyber incidents response activities; (e) issue guidelines, advisories, vulnerability notes and whitepapers relating to information security practices. CERT-In may call for information relating to cyber incidents from any service provider, intermediary, data centre or body corporate, and persons shall comply.

title: Penalty for Breach of Confidentiality and Privacy
content: Save as otherwise provided in this Act or any other law for the time being in force, any person who, in pursuance of any of the powers conferred under this Act, rules or regulations made thereunder, has secured access to any electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material without the consent of the person concerned, discloses such electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees, or with both.

title: Punishment for Disclosure of Information in Breach of Lawful Contract
content: Save as otherwise provided in this Act or any other law for the time being in force, any person including an intermediary who, while providing services under the terms of lawful contract, has secured access to any material containing personal information about another person, with the intent to cause or knowing that he is likely to cause wrongful loss or wrongful gain, discloses, without the consent of the person concerned, or in breach of a lawful contract, such material to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees, or with both. This addresses breach of confidentiality in outsourcing and data processing arrangements.

title: Exemption from Liability of Intermediary (Safe Harbour)
content: (1) Notwithstanding anything contained in any law for the time being in force but subject to the provisions of sub-sections (2) and (3), an intermediary shall not be liable for any third party information, data, or communication link made available or hosted by him. (2) Exemption under sub-section (1) shall apply if: (a) function of intermediary is limited to providing access to communication system and intermediary does not initiate transmission, select receiver, or select/modify information; (b) intermediary observes due diligence while discharging duties under this Act and observes guidelines as prescribed. (3) Exemption shall not apply if: (a) intermediary has conspired or abetted or aided or induced commission of the unlawful act; (b) upon receiving actual knowledge or being notified by Government that any information is being used to commit unlawful act, the intermediary fails to expeditiously remove or disable access. Explanation: 'Third party information' means any information dealt with by intermediary in his capacity as an intermediary. Shreya Singhal: 'Actual knowledge' requires court order or government notification, not mere private notice.

title: Act to Have Overriding Effect
content: The provisions of this Act shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any other law for the time being in force. However, nothing in this Act shall restrict any person from exercising any right conferred under the Copyright Act, 1957 or the Patents Act, 1970. This establishes IT Act as special law overriding general laws in case of conflict.

act: Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021
title: Due Diligence by Intermediary
content: An intermediary shall: (a) publish rules, privacy policy, user agreement informing users not to host, display, upload, modify, publish, transmit, store content which is harmful to children, infringes patent/trademark/copyright, defames, harms minors, impersonates, violates applicable law, threatens public health/safety, deceives users; (b) inform users that non-compliance may result in termination of access; (c) periodically inform users of not hosting prohibited content; (d) include terms requiring users not to host content prohibited under applicable law; (e) inform users that in case of non-compliance, intermediary has right to terminate access and remove non-compliant content; (f) take all reasonable measures to secure computer resource and information; (g) provide information within 72 hours upon order from authorized government agency and other reasonable assistance; (h) preserve and retain information for 180 days or as ordered; (i) not knowingly deploy/install any system or algorithm that modifies or selects content based on user's consent; (j) disable access within 36 hours of receiving court order or government notification; (k) appoint Grievance Officer to acknowledge complaint within 24 hours and resolve within 15 days; (l) enable tracing of first originator of information on requirement from government.

act: Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021
title: Additional Due Diligence for Significant Social Media Intermediary
content: A significant social media intermediary (SSMI) with 5 million+ registered users in India shall additionally: (a) appoint Chief Compliance Officer (CCO) who shall be resident Indian citizen and responsible for ensuring compliance - liable for any non-compliance by intermediary; (b) appoint Nodal Contact Person for 24x7 coordination with law enforcement - resident Indian citizen; (c) appoint Resident Grievance Officer who shall be resident Indian citizen; (d) publish monthly compliance report mentioning details of complaints received and action taken; (e) enable identification of first originator of information on requirement from government where such information is required for prevention, detection, investigation, prosecution or punishment of offences related to sovereignty, security, public order or for offences punishable with imprisonment not less than 5 years; (f) deploy technology-based measures including automated tools for proactive identification and removal of content depicting rape, child sexual abuse, content previously removed upon government or court order; (g) provide users with ability to verify accounts through voluntary verification; (h) ensure prominent display of mark of verification to verified users.
